ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to yuzu juice drink exists; mapped to closest parent class FOODON_03301710 (fruit juice drink) as the most defensible ancestor. Yuzu juice drink is a sweetened diluted citrus juice beverage that falls within this broader category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to yuzu juice drink exists; A0EJW (Fruit nectars and drinks) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy for a sweetened diluted citrus juice beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for yuzu juice drink; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, providing around 115 kcal and 28 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Yuzu Juice Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yuzu juice drink (cr_id D03070038). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for yuzu juice drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 based on the closest analogous entry: orange juice (unsweetened, GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database). Yuzu juice drink is a sweetened citrus juice beverage with similar sugar composition (11.3 g/100 g) and negligible fibre (0.06 g/100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 x 28.2) / 100 = 14.6 for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.26 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage triggers insulin-driven sodium retention and independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (6 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; choose unsweetened yuzu water or plain water with fresh yuzu instead, as the 11.26 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 11.26 g/100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.06 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as its 11.26 g/100 g sugar with virtually no fibre causes rapid glycaemic excursions; unsweetened yuzu squeezed into water is the recommended alternative per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages at this sugar concentration (11.26 g/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased LDL-C, and higher cardiovascular event risk per AHA 2021 dietary guidance.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate this sugar-sweetened beverage, as habitual intake of beverages with more than 10 g sugar per 100 g is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular mortality in prospective cohort studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 11.26 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no qualifying anti-inflammatory nutrient (omega-3, polyphenols, more than 3 g whole-food fibre) is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened drink; fresh unsweetened yuzu juice would be preferable due to its vitamin C and flavonoid content, but the sweetened commercial version provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuzu juice drink is a commercially prepared sweetened citrus beverage made from Japanese yuzu (Citrus junos) fruit concentrate diluted with water and sweeteners. Yuzu is a small, tart citrus fruit native to East Asia, prized for its intensely aromatic, floral-citrus flavour that blends notes of grapefruit, mandarin, and lemon. The drink has a pale yellow colour, a refreshing tart-sweet taste, and a thin, watery texture typical of diluted juice drinks. It is widely consumed in Japan and has gained popularity in Singapore and other Asian markets as a flavoured beverage option. Nutritionally, it provides 46 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (11.28 g/100 g), of which 11.26 g is sugar. Protein (0.12 g/100 g) and fat (0.0 g/100 g) are negligible. Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.06 g/100 g and sodium is low at 6 mg/100 g. The high sugar content with negligible fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite yuzu's natural vitamin C and flavonoid content in its whole-fruit form, the diluted commercial drink provides no meaningful micronutrient benefit. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in yuzu juice drink?
Is yuzu drink good for high blood pressure?
What is yuzu juice made from?
Is yuzu juice drink suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070038) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is an analogous estimate based on orange juice from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest approved-source equivalent for a sweetened citrus juice drink.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03070038; this is consistent with a water-based fruit juice drink containing no added fats or oils. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by formulation (water, fruit concentrate, sweeteners only). calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on orange juice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for yuzu juice drink was found in any approved source. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid_arthritis have been preserved and mapped into health_context with updated condition keys per Veritas v2 rules (diabetes mapped to type2_diabetes). Cardiovascular disease condition added per mandatory Q7 requirement. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per validator requirements.
